summary: Drew DeVault responds to the Microsoft acquisition of GitHub by presenting sr.ht as a fundamentally different alternative rather than a reactionary escape. He argues that most GitHub alternatives merely clone its UX, while sr.ht embraces git's native workflows like email-based patches. The platform prioritizes maintainers over drive-by contributors and rejects venture capital in favor of a subscription-based grassroots model. He plans a cautious, non-disruptive migration of his own projects only when sr.ht is ready.
key_points: Most GitHub alternatives are GitHub clones; sr.ht rethinks project hosting by building on email and git's native strengths
sr.ht optimizes for maintainers and core contributors rather than end-users and drive-by contributors
Venture capital funding misaligns incentives with user needs, so sr.ht uses a subscription-based grassroots model
The platform offers maintainer-focused tools like patch queues, custom ticket searches, and KVM-based CI
Migration of existing projects will happen gradually to avoid disruption
money_quotes: I simply think I can do it better.
Unlike GitLab, Gogs/Gitea, BitBucket; I don't see the GitHub UX as the pinnacle of project hosting.
sr.ht instead embraces git more closely, for example building on top of email rather than instead of email.
GitHub optimizes for the end-user and the drive-by contributor. sr.ht optimizes for the maintainers and core contributors instead.
GitHub and GitLab are both VC-funded and trapped into appeasing their shareholders.
In my opinion, venture capital does not lead to healthy businesses or a healthy economy on the whole, and I think the users suffer for it.
Growth will be artificially slowed if necessary to make sure the infrastructure can keep up.
key_insight: The best alternative to GitHub isn't a GitHub clone but a platform that rethinks project hosting around git's native workflows and aligns its business model with users rather than shareholders.
